coredata.dc:description: The ability to manipulate carbon quantum dots (CQDs) fluorescence with specific substances has been of primary importance for applications in advanced dynamic information encryption. The development of CQDs with specific functionalization, high selectivity and excellent fluorescence stability for information security and sensing remains a challenge. In this paper, we present an N-doped and tryptophan functionalized CQDs that can be applied not only as a fluorescent ink for multiple message encryption but also for fluorescent sensitive and selective recognition of Cu2+ and cysteine (Cys) by multi-method, including aqueous solutions, filter paper, hydrogels and in human DU-145 Prostate cancer cells. Theoretical calculations and mechanistic studies suggested that this on–off-on mechanism was attributed to the transfer of electrons between Cu2+ and functionalized groups on the surface of CQDs and stronger coordination capability with Cys. The above results showed that these newly designed CQDs will greatly enrich the fundamental understanding of CQDs by explaining the recognition mechanism between Cu2+ and Cys, and provide an opportunity for anti-counterfeiting, solid-state fluorescent materials, cellular imaging and environmental.

head.ce:title: Photostable and printable fluorescence carbon quantum dots for advanced message encryption and specific reversible multiple sensing of Cu2+ and cysteine

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The ability to manipulate carbon quantum dots (CQDs) fluorescence with specific substances has been of primary importance for applications in advanced dynamic information encryption. The development of CQDs with specific functionalization, high selectivity and excellent fluorescence stability for information security and sensing remains a challenge. In this paper, we present an N-doped and tryptophan functionalized CQDs that can be applied not only as a fluorescent ink for multiple message encryption but also for fluorescent sensitive and selective recognition of Cu2+ and cysteine (Cys) by multi-method, including aqueous solutions, filter paper, hydrogels and in human DU-145 Prostate cancer cells. Theoretical calculations and mechanistic studies suggested that this on–off-on mechanism was attributed to the transfer of electrons between Cu2+ and functionalized groups on the surface of CQDs and stronger coordination capability with Cys. The above results showed that these newly designed CQDs will greatly enrich the fundamental understanding of CQDs by explaining the recognition mechanism between Cu2+ and Cys, and provide an opportunity for anti-counterfeiting, solid-state fluorescent materials, cellular imaging and environmental.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: The security of vital information has drawn significant attentions in modern society and has driven enormous research efforts in developing of advanced materials for storing and delivering of confidential data [1], for which outstanding fluorescence properties, such as tunable fluorescence emission and excellent fluorescence stability are highly preferred [2,3]. Among the attractive materials, CQDs, as a class of carbon nanomaterial with photoluminescence (PL) performance, has attracted considerable attention and manifold applications in a diversity of areas. Due to numerous superior features including controllable photoluminescent, outstanding aqueous solubility, superior biological safety, facile synthesis and surface modification, CQDs are suitable for information encryption, biosensing, drug delivery, and photocatalysis. [4–8]. Unfortunately, the poor stability and low fluorescent quantum yield extremely limited the utilization of CQDs in various fields [9,10], especially in advanced information encryption and biological imaging [11–14]. To further promote the application value of CQDs, surface modification and heteroatom doping are the most popular and powerful methods in improving the optical properties, recognition capabilities and biocompatibility [15,16]. In comparison to other non-metallic and heavy metal elements, nitrogen has similar atomic radii to carbon which are more likely to displace crystalline carbon. In particular, the lone-pair electrons of nitrogen provide CQDs better affinity and optical properties for detection of electron-deficient targets [17–19]. In achieving of nitrogen doping, the integrated utilization of nitrogen-containing polymers and biomolecules as carbon precursors is an interesting mean to increase doping content and realize surface modification, thus improving the application value of CQDs [20–23].

With effective doping and surface modification, CQDs-based fluorescent probes are up to now capable for detection of metal ions, small molecules, anions, and biologically active small molecules [24,25]. Among a variety of interesting targets, Cu2+ performs a crucial role in various biological processes, including enzyme catalysis, hematopoietic, and protection of the central nervous system [26,27]. Countless proteins employ Cu2+ as a catalyst for redox metabolism, and DNA or RNA synthesis [28,29]. Importantly, some recent results supported that the homeostasis of Cu2+ could also be implicated in the underlying mechanisms of numerous neurological pathologies, particularly in Parkinson’s disease and Alzheimer’s disease [30,31]. In the environment, Cu2+ can cause irreversible hazards to water and soil thus further enter the body of mammals through enrichment effects [32,33]. On the other hand, Cys, an essential intracellular biothiol, plays diverse critical actions in irreversible detoxification reactions and cellular functions in living cells. For example, Cys has an effective detoxification effect on a wide range of toxins such as formaldehyde, acetaldehyde, chloroform, lead, cadmium, chloromethyl mercury, lipid peroxide, etc. [34] The regular values of Cys in healthy serum is 152.8–266.5 μmol·L–1, more and more disease diagnoses have been associated with this Cys clinical concentration, such as skin lesions, lethargy, abatement of fatigue, psoriasis, hair depigmentation, muscle-loss, liver injury, and cancers, etc. [35–37].

Tracking of Cu2+ and Cys is necessary in both physiology process and the environment since which are involved in numerous physiological processes [38,39]. Much efforts have been devoted to develop the fluorescent sensor for Cu2+ and Cys, especially for the Cu2+ and Cys bifunctional sensors. However, most of the reported sensors were based on organic molecular with multiple post-recognition groups. The conjugated π-bond structures of the organic molecular plays a negative role in the water solubility. sensitivity and accuracy of the measurement [40–42]. Therefore, it is significant to explore stable, rapid, sensitive and selective keys to accurately monitor and determine Cu2+ and Cys fluctuations to meet clinical, environmental, and biological microenvironmental requirements [43–46]. Delightfully, Cu2+ and Cys can be simultaneously sensed by a unique on–off-on mechanism stemming from the ligand of specific amino acid residues on the CQDs surface with Cu2+ to quench the fluorescence, while the stronger coordination ability of Cys can reverse the quenching process and recover the fluorescence [47].

Taking these limitations mentioned above into consideration, a bifunctional N-coped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) was synthesized from polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP) and tryptophan (Trp) via green and facile hydrothermal reaction. Data characterization and cellular assay results showed that the synthesized aqueous-soluble N-CQDs has been functionalized by tryptophan residues, which has uniform size, excellent photoluminescence, pH stability, biosafety and cellular labeling competence. Notably, the N-CQDs can be employed as a multi-functional photoluminescent sensor that exhibits great sensitivity and selectivity for Cu2+ and Cys. Due to the unique surface properties and high nitrogen content, N-CQDs was anchored in the hydrogel grid structure for functional detection and powerful adsorption of Cu2+ and Cys. Importantly, The N-CQDs were prepared as fluorescent inks for advanced multiple message encryption because of the fluorescent stability and high quantum yield of CQDs and the unique on–off-on mechanism. In addition, N-CQDs and Cu2+-N-CQDs were successfully applied in Human DU-145 Prostate cancer cells and for real-time monitoring of intracellular Cu2+ and Cys. In this regard, the development of this on–off-on strategy provides relatively inexpensive, sensitive and selective determination of Cu2+ and Cys in biological systems.

PVP and Trp were bought from Aladdin Industrial Co., ltd. (Shanghai, China). All other reagents, including various metallic compounds were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals are of analytical grade and used without further purification.

The fluorescence spectrometer (Horiba, USA) was used to collect the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs and Cu2+-N-CQDs. The absorption spectrum was collected by the UV-1700 spectrophotometer (HITACHI, Japan). The UV1700 spectrophotometer and scale information of the N-CQDs are filmed by the Talos F200X electron microscope (FEI, Czech Republic). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was surveyed on PHI 5000 Versa Probe III spectrometer (ULVAC-PHI, Japan). The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) data of the N-CQDs was made from the Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). TENSOR-27 FTIR spectrophotometer (Bruker, Germany) was used to record Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FT-IR). The transient fluorescence spectrum was obtained by Edinburgh FLS920 (EDINBURGH, UK).

In Scheme 1 , dissolved 0.2 g PVP and 0.5 g Trp in 23 mL of deionized water and ultrasonicated then transferred the solution to 25 mL polytetrafluoroethylene (Teflon) lined stainless steel autoclave. After 12 h of heating at 180 °C and then naturally cooled to natural temperature. Subsequently, the N-CQDs mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 8 min to separate oblivious impurity [48]. Next, the supernatant was dialyzed with 1000 Da dialysis bag for 36 h. The purified dialysis clear liquid was freeze-dried to acquire a cream solid powder and stored in refrigerator (4 °C) for further use [49].

The recognition of Cu2+ and Cys were investigated in aqueous N-CQDs solution at pH = 7.4 at ambient temperature, using PBS. Stock standard solutions of 0.1 mmol·L-1 Cu2+ were made by melting a suitable volume of CuSO4 in aqueous and calibrating the volume to 150 mL in a volumetric flask. Other metal ion solutions were prepared in the same way before use. The fluorescent variation of the N-CQDs was recorded after a range of concentrations of Cu2+ aqueous solution was added to the same amount of N-CQDs (1 mg·L-1) respectively and the mixed solutions were reacted for 10 min. To analyze the selectivity of N-CQDs, their optical responses to various active molecule ions containing Li+, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Cr3+, Al3+, Fe3+, Sn4+, O2 ∙−, H2O2, NO, H2S, ONOO–, ClO–, SO3 2–, AA, GSH, Hcy, Cys, Cu2+ in aqueous solution were investigated via fluorescence spectroscopy [50].

For recognition of Cys, N-CQDs (1 mg·L-1) solution and Cu2+ (0.5 mmol·L-1) solution were mixed in the tubes. After different concentrations of Cys aqueous were added to the mix solution was then added into the tubes. The reaction solution was diluted with ultrapure water to 10 mL and the fluorescence spectra were recorded after shaking well for 15 s. To evaluate the selectivity of Cys, eighteen anions such as AMP, ADP, ATP, O2 ∙−, H2O2, NO, H2S, ONOO–, ClO–, PO4 3–, CO3 2–, OAc–, C2O4 2–, SO3 2–, DA, AA, GSH, Hcy, Cys were as co-existing ions to measure the effect on the fluorescence of Cu2+-N-CQDs system[51]. The fluorescence spectrum was collected subsequently from 355 to 550 nm and the slit width for both excitation and emission was 5 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Cu2+ and Cys solid paper sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The solid sensors were prepared from filter paper cued into strips and N-CQDs. Cu2+ solid sensors were prepared by soaking the filter paper into N-CQDs solution, after air-drying, they were added to the prepared solution of interfering substances at the concentration of 100 μmol·L-1 and placed for half an hour. Unlike the former, Cys solid sensors were prepared by soaking the filter paper into complex solution without fluorescence, followed by immersion in an interference solution for 1 h [52]. Then the solid sensor was taken out and filmed under the UV (365 nm) lamp.

Firstly, the N-CQDs was dissolved into the certain concentration of 10 mL solution, then 27 g of acrylamide (AM) monomer and the initiator ammonium persulfate (APS) were added into well-configured N-CQDs aqueous dispersion. The mixed solution was ultrasound in an ice-water bath for 2–3 min to dissolve AM and APS and mixed well, nitrogen was passed for about 15 min to remove the oxygen from the solution. After that, the mixed solution was quickly injected into the glass mold, and the mouth of the tube was sealed with plastic wrap and ultrasound in the ice-water bath for about 5 min to remove the tiny bubbles in the solution. Finally, the tube with the mixed solution and sealed was placed at 65 °C to obtain the composite hydrogel N-CQDs-PAM [44].

The absorption experiments were performed by adding 0.1 g of freeze-dried N-CQDs-PAM to 100 mL of Cu2+ solution (0.1 g·L-1). The absorption capacity was determined by the equation. Qt = (C0-Ct) *V/m. The parameter V in the equation denotes the volume of the solution and m is the mass of the N-CQDs-PAM. C0 denotes the incipient concentration of the Cu2+, and Ct is the immediate concentration of Cu2+.

8 mL of N-CQDs dispersion was measured with a concentration of 33 mg·mL−1 in a 50 mL beaker, then added 8 mL of ethanol and stir well. Dissolve 540 mg sodium carboxymethylcellulose into beaker with 60 mL of deionized water, next was placed in an oil bath at 85 °C with constant stirring (350 r·min−1) for 20 min. Then the 250 mg of polyvinylpyrrolidone solution we prepared was dissolved into 20 mL of deionized water and stirred well [53].
The final ratio was: 3 mL of N-CQDs solution and 7.5 mL of ethanol, 14.80 g of N-CQDs/ethanol dispersion, 3.7 g of CMC solution, 1 mL of polyvinylpyrrolidone stirred well to obtain fluorescent ink.

The viability of MCF-7 cells was determined by the methyl thiazolyl tetrazolium (MTT) assay [54]. Firstly, seeded cells (5000 cells per well) were plated in 96-well dishes and incubated in RPMI Medium Modified, HyClone with 10 % gibco FBS (Art. No. 10270) and 1 % hydrostreptomycin at 37 °C under 5 % CO2. Various concentrations (0, 200, 400, 600, 800 and 1000 mg·mL−1) of N-CQDs were added to the cells after 24 h of culturing and incubated for another 24 h and then added to microtiter plate and incubated for 24 h. Microplate readers were used to measure the absorbance at 490 nm of every well.

Fluorescent imaging in living Human DU-145 Prostate cancer cells was performed in similar procedures [55]. In a confocal dish, Human DU-145 Prostate cancer cells were plated, and 1 mg·L-1 N-CQDs were added after the cells adhered, the cells were then incubated at 37 °C for 4 h. After the culturing, in the Cu2+ detection group, after the cells were washed three times by PBS, cells were imaged after 30 min at 37 °C in a growth medium replenished with 100 μmol·L-1 Cu2+. The cells were continued to be incubated with 100 μmol·L-1 Cys in the culture medium at 37 °C for 30 min and imaged based on Cu2+ treated cells.

In order to careful observation, the microstructure and lattice spacing of N-CQDs were characterized by TEM and high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM). The pattern (Fig. 1 a) showed that the resultant CQDs had an almost circular geometry with a particle size distribution between 1.5 and 4.5 nm and a well-distributed balance particle scale of 2.92 ± 0.17 nm by counting more than 150 nanoparticles (Fig. 1c). The HRTEM images displayed that N-CQDs have an obvious lattice spacing of around 0.21 nm (Fig. 1b) that can be according to the (100) plane of the graphene carbon core. The atomic force microscopy (AFM) image showed that the N-CQDs have a height of ∼2.5 nm (Fig. S10), as well as demonstrated the monodispersed and quasi-spherical morphology of N-CQDs with uniform distribution. The XRD pattern (Fig. 1d) showed a broad peak at 2θ angle of 22.1° (0.40 nm), implying the amorphous nature of N-CQDs [56]. Furthermore, the tiny gap in interlayer spacing between N-CQDs and graphite was attributed to the occurrence of extensive functional regiments at the borderline of N-CQDs, which widen the interlayer spacing of graphene (002) (0.34 nm) [57]. The typical Raman spectrum (Fig. 1e) was recorded in the D band at 1326 cm−1 and the G band at 1584 cm−1. Individually, the D band depicted the disorder microstructures or sp3 defects of N-CQDs, and the G band referred to the graphitic sp2 carbon clusters. Moreover, the ratio of sp3/sp2 carbon specified was computed to be 0.77 from the relative intensity of ID/IG [58].

To survey the functional group and composition of N-CQDs, FT-IR investigation was performed. The FT-IR spectroscopy of N-CQDs (Fig. 1f) in the range of 3414 cm−1 and 2954 cm−1 presented the stretching vibrational bands of OH, NH and CH. The peak of 1658 cm−1 was originated from the CO while the CC stretching vibration was found at the 1495 cm−1. The peak in 1427 cm−1 was associated with the asymmetric stretching vibration of the carboxylate anions, and the strong peaks at 1290 cm−1 and 1230 cm−1 coincide perfectly with the CN stretching vibration, which suggested the presence of an amide bond, indicating a functionalizing effect of tryptophan on the N-CQDs surface [59].

To further elaborate the elemental composition and the chemical valence bonding mode of the surface group of N-CQDs, the XPS spectra (Fig. 2 d) of N-CQDs was measured. In brief, the XPS image demonstrated that the N-CQDs has three distinct bond energy peaks, which are located at 285 eV (C1s), 399 eV (N1s) and 530 eV (O1s), representing the N-CQDs are composed of C (53.23 %), N (7.47 %), and O (11.07 %). The C1s spectrum (Fig. 2a) can be decomposed to four peaks: CC/CC at 285.7 eV and further demonstrated that the CC component is the dominant carbon-binding configuration and the N-CQDs has a graphitic matrix structure, 284.8 eV, 286.3 eV and 288.2 eV was designated as CN, CO/CN and COOH. In the high-resolution O1s (Fig. 2b) spectrum, the peaks of 531.3 eV and 530.3 eV were linked to CO and CO. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) was resolved into peaks of 399.4 eV, 398.9 eV and 398.2 eV indicative of the existence of NH, CN and CN [60,61]. The 1H NMR spectra (Fig. S11) of the N-CQDs show sp2-hybridized carbon atoms. The Ha′ atoms exhibit homologous chemical shifts to the Ha atoms of Trp. The N-CQDs of COOH, CN, CO, CH, NH and OH in FT-IR (Fig. S12) exhibited homology with Trp. All of the above results supported the high nitrogen doping of N-CQDs synthesized via PVP and Trp as precursors.

The optical properties of N-CQDs were determined by the UV–vis absorption spectrum, fluorescent excitation and emission spectra, lifetime and quantum yield. The UV–vis (Fig. 2e) spectrum of N-CQDs showed that there is one absorption peak centered at 270 nm, which was correlated with the n-π* transition of CO groups [62]. To further characterize the fluorescence intensity (FL) features of the N-CQDs, a range of emission spectra for the N-CQDs were documented at a variety of excitation wavelengths (300–400 nm) (Fig. 2f). The results exhibited the excitation independent emission with the maximum fluorescent emission at 442 nm with excitation of 340 nm. Additionally, the N-CQDs fluorescence lifetime (Fig. S1) was measured to be 9.26 ns by an equation of τ = τ1B1 + τ2B2, where B1, B2 stand for fractional intensities; τ1, τ2 are decay times. The average fluorescence lifetime was calculated from the following formula. Using quinine sulfate as standard, the relative quantum yield of the N-CQDs were obtained as 29.2 % by the following equation:

where φ stands for the fluorescence quantum yield, F refers to the integrated fluorescence intensity, and A is the absorption value. The subscript “R” and “S” correspond to the reference and the sample.

The sustained stability of fluorescence was prominent problem for previous FL materials. We measured the fluorescence stability of N-CQDs under different NaCl concentrations and pH, strong Xe and UV light and long storage time. The fluorescence properties of N-CQDs exhibited no significant changes at a series of NaCl (0–1 mol·L-1) concentrations (Fig. S2). Not only that, the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs remains stable at pH 1.0–13.0 (Fig. S3) explaining the ability to recognize targeting goals under various unique conditions. The fluorescence properties of N-CQDs were undisturbed for continuous long-time under both Xe (1500 mW·cm−2) and UV (365 nm) lamp irradiation for up to 1.5 h (Fig. S4 and 5) suggesting their superior resistance to photobleaching capability. More importantly, the fluorescence properties of solid N-CQDs maintained a high level after storage for at least 6 months (Fig. S6). Thermogravimetric analysis (TG) and the derivative thermogravimetric analysis (DTG) (Fig. S7) were used to analyze the thermal stability of N-CQDs, which exhibited a typical one-step decomposition process, the first component lost below 150 °C is likely water of crystallization. The second degradation at 150–380 °C was ascribed to decomposition from groups (amide, hydroxy, unsaturated double bonds, ether bonds, etc.) on the surface of N-CQDs. The last degradation from 390 to 600 °C was described to species that are linked strongly to the N-CQDs via covalent bonds or some active parts of the carbon cores. On the other hand, the fluorescence intensity remains constant from 25 to 100 °C (Fig. S8). Interestingly, the Fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs solutions was stable even at low temperatures of 0-20 °C (Fig. S9). The above finding demonstrates that N-CQDs can sustain excellent fluorescence stability as photoluminescent sensor even in complex systems and are believed to have great prospects in message encryption, biosensing and imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cu2+ by the N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Numerous CQDs were previously used to detect Hg2+, Fe3+ and Pb2+, we introduced reactive groups such as amino, carboxyl and carbonyl groups into the products by PVP and Trp during the synthesis for detecting Cu2+. Thus, in anticipation of verifying the selectivity of N-CQDs to Cu2+, we tested the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs at 442 nm in the presence of number of ions and active molecules. Conspicuous weak emission was noticed contributing to the vigorous quenching agent of Cu2+ solution at 442 nm. Other metal ions induced no distinctive effect on the fluorescence of the N-CQDs (Fig. 3 a). Moreover, in Fig. 3a, when active molecules and other ions (Fig. 3a, #2−#29, #1: blank) coexist with Cu2+, which can also validly quench the fluorescence of N-CQDs. In addition, the quenched intensity is not influenced by coexisting anions and active molecules. The above experimental data indicated that N-CQDs has the potential to detect Cu2+ with high selectivity and anti-interference in the real sample.

To obtain better detection effects, we optimized the conditions related to the CQDs detection of Cu2+. We chose concentration of 1 mg·L-1 for the N-CQDs in order to achieve a lower detection limit. We further determined the reaction kinetics of CQDs. When 200 μmol·L-1 Cu2+ was added, the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs quenched rapidly within 1.5 min (Fig. S13). Additionally, we discussed the reactivity of Cu2+ and N-CQDs at different pH (1–13), as shown in Fig. 3c, N-CQDs has a satisfactory quenching effect by adding Cu2+ when the pH is 5–12, this pH range encompasses most of the practical application samples and cells. Considering the cellular circumstances, we chased pH = 7.8 for the subsequent experimental conditions. All in all, we finally selected 1 mg·L-1N-CQDs, pH = 5, and a reaction time of 1.5 min as the best conditions for recognizing Cu2+. Under these conditions, we evaluated the capability of N-CQDs to detect Cu2+ quantitatively. As shown in Fig. 3b, the FL intensity of N-CQDs gradually reduced with the concentration increasing of Cu2+ (0.01–200 μmol·L-1). The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 0.023 μmol·L-1 (Table S2) which is less than the concentration levels for the recently reported sensor for Cu2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cys by the Cu2+-N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Cys is one of the most common biological thiols and is used as a metal ion detoxifier due to the strong complexing ability of its sulfhydryl group. As the contains Cu2+ on the surface N-CQDs, we suppose the Cu2+-N-CQDs system can be used to quantify Cys. As expected, the FL intensity of N-CQDs quenched by Cu2+ could be recovered by adding Cys to the Cu2+-N-CQDs solution (Fig. 3d). The fluorescence restores ratio reached up to 99.1 % upon the addition of Cys at the concentration of 400 μmol·L-1. We determined the reaction kinetics of Cu2+-N-CQDs, when 400 μmol·L-1 Cys was added, the FL intensity of N-CQDs will rapidly recover, and the quenching will be entirely within 30 s (Fig. S14). Cu2+-N-CQDs has a satisfactory FL recovery effect by adding Cys when the pH was 4–12 (Fig. 3f).

The Cu2+-N-CQDs exhibited outstandingly selectivity to Cys. The addition of various other active biomolecules which including AMP, ADP, ATP, O2 ∙−, H2O2, NO, H2S, ONOO–, ClO–, PO4 3–, CO3 2–, OAc–, C2O4 2–, DA, SO3 2–, AA, GSH, Hcy could not trigger any significant fluorescence fluctuation (Fig. 3d, #1-#18, #19: blank). When we added the Cys from the concentration of 0.001 to 400 μmol·L-1, the fluorescence intensity at 442 nm displayed 20–fold recovery after the reaction of Cu2+-N-CQDs. A favorable linear relationship was found with correlation coefficient of 0.996 when the Cys amount varied in the series of 0.01–300 μmol·L-1 (Fig. 3e). The LOD was computed to be 0.045 μmol·L-1 (Table S2). Finally, it was observed that the other coexisting anions exhibited no obvious interference compared with Cys. This Cu2+-N-CQDs system has higher sensitivity and anti-interference capabilities compared to traditional testing mechanisms (Table S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of N-CQDs for the solid sensor to recognize Cu2+ and Cys
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of solid-phase detectors enhances the applicability and efficiency. N-CQDs are uniformly distributed in filter paper and hydrogel networks to produce solid sensors due to their mini size and astonishing optical stability under many sophisticated conditions. The N-CQDs doped papers (Fig. 4 a) demonstrated bright blue fluorescence and could quickly sense aqueous Cu2+, while other typical small molecules and metal ions have no distinct effect. Moreover, the Cu2+-N-CQDs doped papers (Fig. 4b) indicated superior selectivity for Cys over other sulfur-containing molecules. The elastic lattice structure of hydrogels provides space for doping with N-CQDs to synthesize 3D material (Fig. 4e) to achieve track and absorption of Cu2+ in water. N-CQDs was doped into transparent ammonium persulfate-acrylamide hydrogels and employed in sensing experiments (Fig. 4c). Compared to the non-fluorescence of the pure hydrogels, the N-CQDs doped hydrogel represented an intense fluorescence that can be dramatically quenched upon the addition of Cu2+ within 200 min (Fig. 4f). The equilibrium adsorption capacity of the N-CQDs-PAM in Cu2+ solution was 65.5 mg·g−1 (Fig. S16). At the same time, the blue color of the aqueous Cu2+ solution gradually cleared (Fig. 4d). In summary, the N-CQDs-doped solid-phase sensor substantially improved the efficiency of environmental detection of Cu2+ and Cys, and the N-CQDs-PAM hydrogel also provided a novel strategy to solve the contamination of Cu2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of N-CQDs for advanced message encryption
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of materials that can be encrypted and decrypted multiple times has long been a subject of interest. In this paper, the on–off-on N-CQDs was synthesized to achieve multiple encryptions of information using fluorescence. As the first step of encryption, the encrypted information (numbers (Fig. 5, a1-a4 ), English (Fig. 5, b1-b4), Chinese (Fig. 5, c1-c4), QR Code (Fig. 5, d1-d4)) were printed on the paper with N-CQDs based fluorescent ink, and the information cannot be viewed under sunlight. The encrypted content is decrypted for the first time by irradiation with a 365 nm UV lamp. The second encryption step uses a Cu2+ solution to spray the written content on the paper several times, and the encrypted information cannot be observed either under sunlight or UV (365 nm) irradiation. For the next decryption, the paper was sprayed several times using the Cys solution for the second decryption and then dried. Under 365 nm UV lamp, the blue glowing information was restored and visible to the naked eye. Respectively, the printed fluorescence QR Code (Fig. 5, e1-e4) can also be used as an anti-counterfeiting tool for conventional information derived from reversible changes in fluorescence. Therefore, this low cost and reliable on–off-on strategy has potential for application in multi-level information encryption and anti-counterfeiting.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of N-CQDs for Human DU-145 Prostate cancer cells
ce:section.ce:para: The ability to track Cu2+ and Cys under biological conditions is essential for the N-CQDs [63]. Firstly, the cytotoxicity of N-CQDs to MCF-7 cells was evaluated by standard MTT experiment. Over 90 % of the cells were viable (Fig. S15) with the N-CQDs at concentrations up to 1000 mg·mL−1. The next experiments explored the capability of N-CQDs to sense Cu2+ and Cys in Human DU-145 Prostate cancer cells using confocal microscope. In accordance with Fig. 6 , Human DU-145 Prostate cancer cells were non-fluorescent in the bright field of view, and bright blue fluorescence was observed in the cells after incubation with N-CQDs for 30 min at 37 °C. In addition, the bright fluorescence emission was mainly located in the cytoplasm as seen in the superimposed plots. However, there is almost no fluorescence signal in the DU-145 cells when the cultivated cells were handled with Cu2+ for 30 min at 37 °C. By contrast, the blue fluorescence of N-CQDs was almost restored after treatment with 300 μmol·L-1 of Cys into cells. The process in which the fluorescence could be quenched in the cells by Cu2+ and restored by Cys demonstrate that the N-CQDs can realize the trace of Cu2+ and Cys at the biological level.

To get insights into the interaction mechanism, theoretical calculation, FT-IR, UV spectra, XPS, lifetimes, and Zeta potential variation were determined to survey the change in N-CQDs with the addition of Cu2+ and Cys. Because of the abundant functional groups of tryptophan residues on the surface of N-CQD, it readily forms coordination complexes with metal ions [64–67].

FT-IR (Fig. 7 a) analysis illustrated that the increase of Cu2+ resulted in a decrease in the 1658 cm−1 peak area of CO and a sharp increase in the peak 1230 cm−1 area of CO, and the carboxyl group essentially disappeared at the 1427 cm−1, indicating that Cu2+ did complex with the carbonyl group of N-CQDs. The new peak at 619 nm is attributed to the stretching vibration of the Cu–O. The new peaks at 500–800 nm indicate the formation of copper complexes by comparing the UV absorption spectra (Fig. S17) of N-CQDs solution before the addition of Cu2+. Respectively, both the Cu–O absorption peak in the UV-absorption spectrum and the Cu–O peak in the FT-IR spectrum appeared to be reduced when Cys was introduced into the system, which is attributed to the fact that most of the Cu2+ was ligated with Cys by exfoliation from the N-CQDs surface. Moreover, the fluorescence on–off–on mechanism of N-CQDs was further explored by XPS. In the O1s spectrum (Fig. 7f), the peak area ratio of CO at 535.4 eV and CO at 533.8 eV increased from 0.94 to 1.1 which result is compatible with FT-IR. In the N1s spectrum (Fig. 7d), the intensity of the amine peak decreased significantly and shifted from 398.7 eV to 401.4 eV. This indicates that more electrons from N and O elements were involved in the coordination of Cu2+, and the amino, amide, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups from Trp and PVP become active sites involved in the recognition of Cu2+. Further, there are clear peaks of Cu elements on the surface of N-CQDs from XPS spectra (Fig. 7e): peaks with binding energies of 932.4 eV and 952.2 eV represented Cu2p3/2 and Cu2p1/2. Deconvolution of the Cu2p3/2 spectrum produced two peaks with binding energies of 933.5 eV and 932.3 eV. The presence of three satellite peaks of 960.3 eV, 941.4 eV, and 938.1 eV proved that elemental Cu exists mostly in the form of Cu2+. Therefore, the two peaks of Cu2p2/3 were designated as Cu2+ in the form of an electron-rich environment [68] that was provided by Trp binding on the surface of the N-CQDs. To further characterize the changes of various substances during the off–on process, XPS spectra of Cys-Cu2+-N-CQDs (Fig. 7g) was investigated and S element was founded. The partial XPS spectrum of S2p (Fig. 7h) showed two peaks 164.3 eV and 165.4 eV which represent the spin–orbit splitting of S2p3/2 and S2p1/2 as a result of the coordination of Cu2+. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 7i) shows one peak at 420.0 eV (Cu–N). As a result, it can be seen that Cys captures the Cu2+ from the N-CQDs surface [69] and induce fluorescent recovery.

The fluorescence lifetimes of N-CQDs and Cu2+-N-CQDs were measured to be 9.26 ns and 5.36 ns (Fig. 7c), which proves that the quenching process is a dynamic quenching process. The fluorescence lifetime returned to 8.04 ns when Cys was added. The test result of Zeta potential (Fig. 7b) shifted from −12.05 mV to 8.42 mV in the presence of Cu2+, which supported that Cu2+ coordinated with the amide group groups of CQDs by electrostatic interaction. Finally, when the Cys were added into the Cu2+-N-CQDs solution, the Zeta potential decreased to −11.60 mV which suggested Cu2+ detached from N-CQDs to bind with Cys upon its introduction.

In order to reveal that only Cys can restore the fluorescence of Cu2+-N-CQDs among the three common biological thiols of Cys, GSH and Hcy, we used theoretical calculations to calculate the activation energy of the coordination process between the three thiols and copper ions. The results (Table S7) are consistent with the experiments that Cys has lower activation energy for coordination with Cu2+, implying that Cys has a stronger coordination ability.

In summary, we synthesized the multifunctional N-CQDs with bright blue fluorescence and performed a comprehensive characterization through a convenient, facile and low-cost hydrothermal approach. The fluorescence of N-CQDs can be quenched by Cu2+ owing to ligand-induced electron transfer, which builds the foundation of Cu2+ quantification. On the other hand, the quenched fluorescence can be recovered quantitatively by Cys contributing to the stronger binding ability of sulfhydryl groups on Cys. This reversible mechanism was successfully used for information encryption and anti-counterfeiting of N-CQDs based fluorescence ink. Encouragingly, N-CQDs based fluorescence solid sensors, such as N-CQDs doped filter papers, and hydrogels, enabled the detection and removal excessive of Cu2+ and Cys in the environment. Moreover, it can be concluded from cell experiments that N-CQDs can be sensitively, selectively, and quickly track trace Cu2+and Cys. In all, this synthesis strategy could provide a novel method for the synthesis of low-cost and sensitive nanosensors in Cu2+and Cys analysis and advanced information storage.
